item.title: #518: Celebrating Django's 20th Birthday With Its Creators
Build Your Own Low-Cost Cloud Backup with Hetzner Storage Boxes
item.author: Developer Service
item.content:encoded:
Cloud storage is convenient, but it can get expensive fast. I wanted a simple, reliable, and for my projects without getting locked into Google Drive or Dropbox pricing.That’s where come in: a generous amount of storage, WebDAV support out of the box, and a price that’s hard to beat.In this article, I’ll show you how I set up my own “personal backup drive” using Hetzner Storage Boxes, with a focus on (local → remote only). This way, I always have an up-to-date backup of my important folders without worrying about accidental remote deletions syncing back to my laptop.
Why Hetzner Storage Boxes? When I started looking for a backup solution, cost and flexibility were the two main factors. Hetzner Storage Boxes checked both boxes. They start at just a few euros per month and provide a generous amount of space, making them one of the most affordable options for long-term storage.Some of the highlights include: – plans start at 1 TB and scale up as needed.Multiple access protocols – WebDAV, FTP, SFTP, and rsync are all supported out of the box. – snapshots and backup intervals can be configured to add extra safety. – far cheaper than popular services like Google Drive, Dropbox, or OneDrive for the same capacity.Below is a price comparison for individual plans, as of mid-2025:Hetzner Storage Box (BX11)Hetzner Storage Box (BX21)
What This Means for Your Wallet Ridiculously low entry cost – Hetzner gives you 1 TB for just over €3/month, versus €9–10 from Google, Dropbox, or Microsoft for similar or even less storage. That’s roughly for comparable space. – Hetzner includes , optional snapshot features, and support for protocols like WebDAV, FTP, SFTP, and rsync. – Paying just a few euros monthly makes it perfect for low-cost backups, especially if your goal is simplicity and affordability.Because WebDAV is natively supported, it integrates seamlessly with Python. That means I can create a simple, lightweight sync solution, like the one in this guide, without relying on heavy third-party tools or vendor lock-in.
Hetzner Storage Box Configuration You can signup for Hetzner here.Hetzner offers several , depending on your needs:Once you’ve got your Storage Box, you’ll need a few details for the scripts in this guide to work: (normally looks like ) for your main account (note: this script doesn’t support sub-accounts) and enabledHere’s an example configuration:And if you’d like to go beyond just Storage Boxes, Hetzner also offers at a budget-friendly price. I’ve put together a PDF guide that shows you how to turn one into your own Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS). A great next step if you want to self-host projects, apps or services, all without breaking the bank.
First Attempt: File Monitoring Only My first approach was the simplest one: just watch my local folders for changes and push any new or modified files to my Hetzner Storage Box. The idea was to keep my backup updated automatically without having to manually run anything or do a full sync each time.Here’s how I thought about it:Monitor my local folder for new or changed files (including moves and deletes).Upload these files changes to the remote storage via WebDAV.Here’s the initial Python script :I store my WebDAV credentials in a file, which the script loads using . This keeps sensitive information separate from the code and easy to update.The script uses to monitor the local folder for changes. Whenever a file is created, modified, deleted, or moved, the corresponding action is applied to the remote folder. This ensures the backup mirrors my local folder accurately.Files are uploaded using a helper function that preserves relative paths, so the remote folder structure stays the same as my local one. Moves and renames are handled intelligently to avoid duplicates, and deletions remove files from the remote storage to keep it clean.The function sets up the WebDAV client, ensures the local and remote directories exist, and starts the Watchdog observer. A loop runs indefinitely, and I can stop the sync anytime with .
Improved Approach: Add Startup Sync It was a good first attempt for basic incremental backups, but I realized that if I wanted a clean and reliable remote mirror, I needed to add a step before entering monitoring mode. That’s where the improved approach comes in:This function works in three phases. First, it walks through my local folder, collecting a complete list of files and directories. At the same time, it queries the Hetzner Storage Box recursively to build a list of what’s already on the remote side.With both lists in hand, the script compares them. Any files or directories that exist remotely but not locally are . This prevents the remote backup from filling up with stale or renamed files. Conversely, any folders that exist locally but not remotely are to keep the directory structure in sync.Finally, the script uploads all local files that are either missing from the remote or have a . That way, I don’t waste time re-uploading unchanged files, but I know everything important is up to date.Once this one-time sync is done, the script switches into using Watchdog, just like before. From that point on, only changes are pushed, which keeps the sync efficient and fast.The end result is a setup where my Hetzner Storage Box always starts as a clean copy of my local folder, and then stays updated in real time. This gives me confidence that my remote backup is accurate, not just “close enough.”Once I had the script ready, I set it up with just a few simple steps.First, I installed the required Python packages. I’m using for file monitoring, for WebDAV communication, and for environment variables:pip watchdog webdavclient3 python-dotenv
Configure Environment Variables Next, I created a file in the same directory as the script. This keeps my credentials safe and out of the code:Finally, I just start the sync process:python hetzner_drive_sync.py On startup, the script performs a to ensure my remote backup matches my local folder. After that, it switches into monitoring mode, watching for changes in real time and syncing them to Hetzner automatically:Local directory: D:itHubetzner-Storage-Box-Cloud-DriveetznerDrive Remote directory: /HetznerDrive Ensuring remote directory exists: /HetznerDrive Starting initial sync... Found 1 files and 1 directories Found 1 remote files and 1 remote directories Deleting remote file not : s/r.txt Deleted 1 remote files Deleted 0 remote directories Created 1 remote directories Uploading: s/b Uploaded 1 files Initial completed! Starting file Ctrl+C to stop... Uploading a -> /HetznerDrive/a Successfully uploaded: a I can stop the sync at any time with .At the end of the day, this little script gives me exactly what I wanted: an affordable personal cloud backup that I fully control. Hetzner’s Storage Box is inexpensive, fast, and reliable, and by layering on a simple one-way sync I don’t have to worry about anything getting out of hand.The key for me is that it’s . My local folder is always the source of truth, and the remote just mirrors it. That means no confusing conflicts, no messy merge logic. Just a clean backup of my files in the cloud.Every time I start the script, it does a full sync to tidy things up, then quietly runs in the background, catching changes as they happen. For me, that adds up to real : my important files are safe off-site, but I don’t have to think about them.

item.title: DAY: 5 - In Python all list method operators and slicing:
item.author: Ashok Kumar
item.content:encoded: Today, I completed learning List Methods and Slicing in Python. Both are very important fundamentals in Python programming.Python provides several built-in methods to work with lists effectively:append → adds an item at the end of the listextend → adds multiple items to the listinsert → inserts an item at a specific positionremove → removes the first matching elementpop → removes an element by index (default is the last element)clear → empties the list completelysort → arranges elements in ascending orderreverse → reverses the order of elements in the listSlicing is the process of extracting a portion of a list (or string) using index values.[:] → returns the full list[start:end] → extracts elements from start to end-1[::step] → extracts with a step size (skips elements)Negative indexes allow slicing from the end of the list.I successfully understood List Methods and Slicing today ✅ This strengthens my Python fundamentals, and tomorrow I will continue with Dictionaries.

item.title: Daniel Roy Greenfeld: TIL: Using SQLModel Asynchronously with FastAPI (and Air) with PostgreSQL
item.content:encoded:
First, let's set up our environment. We'll need to install the necessary packages:uvvenv uvaddSQLModelasyncpgpsycopg2-binarygreenlet A quick quick guide to these dependencies:: The web framework we'll be using. We install with the [standard] extras to make running in the shell easy: The ORM for interacting with our SQL database: A PostgreSQL adapter for Python, which will be used by SQLModel for synchronous operations: An asynchronous PostgreSQL client library, which will be used by SQLModel for async operations: A dependency for SQLAlchemy, which powers SQLModel, to support asynchronous operations.Yes, we need BOTH synchronous and asynchronous PostgreSQL drivers. SQLModel (and SQLAlchemy) use the synchronous driver for certain operations, even when we are mostly working in an async context.Here we'll set up a simple FastAPI application that uses SQLModel asynchronously (and synchronously). As far as I can tell, to create tables we need to use a synchronous engine, which explains the need for both drivers and engines. Specifically we'll create (synchronously) a model and two asynchronous endpoints: one for retrieving stories and another for creating a new story.When creating my example database, I used 'drg' as my username and called the database 'my-database'. Adjust the connection strings as needed for your setup.python
fromfastapiimportDepends,FastAPI fromsqlalchemy.ext.asyncioimportcreate_async_engine,async_sessionmaker fromsqlmodel.ext.asyncio.sessionimportAsyncSession fromsqlmodelimportSQLModel,,Field,create_engine fromtypingimportAsyncGenerator
create_engine,Truecreate_async_engine,True,True, async_sessionmakerasync_engine, AsyncSession, False,
asyncdef_get_async_session->AsyncGeneratorAsyncSession,None: asyncwithasync_sessionassession: yieldsession
Depends_get_async_session classStorySQLModel,True: id:intFieldNone,Trueslug:str
SQLModel.metadata.create_allsync_engineFastAPI
@app.get asyncdefindexsession:get_async_session: Storyawaitsession.execstmt:x.slugxstories
@app.post asyncdefcreate_storyslug:str,session:get_async_session: Storyslugsession.addstoryawaitsession.commit:story.slugAir is a FastAPI-based framework for building HTML-focused web applications. It builds on top of FastAPI and adds features like easier templating, improved form handling, and more. You can combine it with FastAPI routes and SQLModel to create the web pages for your API application.In any case, except for single import change, as well as defining the app and endpoints, the code is identical to the FastAPI example above. The main difference is that instead of returning JSON responses, we return HTML using Air's components and layouts. Air can return Jinja-powered responses, web but here we'll use Air's built-in components for simplicity.First let's set up our environment. We'll need to install the necessary packages:uvvenv uvaddSQLModelasyncpgpsycopg2-binarygreenlet : The FastAPI-powered web framework we'll be using. We install with the [standard] extras to make running in the shell easy: The ORM for interacting with our SQL database: A PostgreSQL adapter for Python, which will be used by SQLModel for synchronous operations: An asynchronous PostgreSQL client library, which will be used by SQLModel for async operations: A dependency for SQLAlchemy, which powers SQLModel, to support asynchronous operations.Now for the code. When creating my example database, I used 'drg' as my username and called the database 'my-database'. Adjust the connection strings as needed for your setup.

item.title: Introducing ConnectOnion: The Simplest Way to Build AI Agents with Python Functions
item.author: Jesse Dong
item.content:encoded:
Introducing Connect Onion — Build AI Agents with Just Python Functions Connect Onion is a lightweight agent framework for Python that transforms regular functions into powerful AI tools.It focuses on simplicity, transparency, and speed, no verbose chains, no overengineered agent classes, no role hierarchies. Just tools, memory, and prompts.Connect Onion solves the pain points developers often face with agent SDKs like LangChain, AutoGen, and CrewAI: — no decorators, no schemas to define from function annotations , memory, trust, and iteration limits decorator to visualize agent thinking for rapid local iteration Fully compatible with OpenAI LLMsOther frameworks introduce layers of abstraction that make agents harder to debug and maintain. Connect Onion takes the opposite approach. Start simple, and layer complexity only if you need it.
Example: Your First Agent Here’s how to go from function to agent in 5 lines:That’s a working agent. It auto-generates a tool schema based on your function signature. You can add memory, change system prompts, or customise its behaviour with zero boilerplate.Max iterations, trust filterThe project is currently in and under active development. Feedback, issues, and contributions are more than welcome!Connect Onion is for developers who want to connect AI to their apps without learning an entire new framework. If you can write a Python function, you can build an agent.

item.title: Building Chrome Dinosaur Game in Pygame (Part 3: Running Dino)
item.author: Chukwuemeka Ngumoha
item.content:encoded:
In the last post, we coded up the infinite scrolling horizon upon which the Dino runs and in the process we learned how to display images on the canvas, how coordinates work in , how to use the class in controlling the of the video game among a few other lessons. In this post, we'll code up the Dino and give it the ability to run for dear life.But before we go any further, I need to make it clear that I'm assuming the following things about you:You understand basic English. Trust me, this is not a given.You possess basic knowledge of the Python programming language. If not, check out this coding resource to learn.You're following along on a Mac, Linux or Windows Laptop or Desktop. You can't use your smartphone for this I'm afraid.You can comfortably change the Refresh rate of our game without breaking a sweat. If you can't, then quickly checkout the previous post before proceeding with this one.
Who left a dinosaur on the open road ? In our previous post, we left our code base looking something like this (adjust your code to match what is displayed):So, the first order of business today will be to display the Dino on the canvas. And using what we did in the previous post as a reference, it's clear we're talking about displaying an image using the method. So, let's do that:A lot of this code should be familiar to you by now. But if you take a closer look, you'll notice that we passed the method as an argument to a pygame.transform.scale(...) method. This method does what it's name suggests: Scales the width and height of the loaded image to meet specific values which we passed in as a tuple of the and constants defined above, both set to a value of . You'll also notice that the position of the Dino on the axis is also calculated based on the initial from the last post and assigned to a constant which is used when displaying the image loaded earlier through the use of . This is so that the Dino will appear as though it were standing on the horizonWhen you run the program, you'll get something that looks like this:Wow, our Dino looks good !!!But it does not seem to be moving. Actually, it's be standing still while the rest of the world moves past it. We don't want that, so how do we fix it ?Essentially, all we need to do is create the illusion of motion. And, the best way to do that is by getting it's legs to move. But, how do you get the legs of an image to move ? Is that even possible ?Well, it's not. But we can switch between two images of the Dino so fast that it would appear as though the Dino were indeed moving it's legs. And luckily for you, we have two images in the directory in our project that can be useful in achieving this:Assets/sprites/Dino_Left_Run.png: An image of the Dino with it's left leg extended.Assets/sprites/Dino_Right_Run.png: An image of the Dino with it's right leg extended.And we'll use both of these to achieve our goal soon. But right now, you may be thinking: how do we signal to the Dinosaur to switch between two images of itself fast enough to create the illusion of motion ?In order to achieve this, we need to learn about two new ideas:An event in is a message that tells your program something has happened — like a key being pressed, the mouse moving, or the game window closing. You can think of it as a notification system: whenever a specific action occurs, Pygame generates an event. By listening for these events, your program knows when and how to respond.We've actually already worked with events in our code:In the example above, we were listening for the event which tells us when the user hits the ❌ on the game window. comes with a lot more events that we can use and it also gives us the ability to define custom events when the need arises:where is any number from upwards. At the end of the day, is really just a constant containing a unique number. So, when we add to that number, we're creating a more unique number to act as our event ID. That's essentially, how that line works behind the scenes. Now, can now be caught within the event checker loop like all other events:
Repeating events with Earlier on, we used the class to create an instance which used the instance method to control the of our game. So, now let's take a brief look at it's cousin: The method basically takes two arguments:An Event: The event we want to repeatA number: The time in milliseconds over which that event is repeatedAnd then will emit the specified event for every interval of time as specified by the second argument:This is really convenient because it's exactly what we need in order to make our program work as intended:A few things to take note of:We use the variable to determine when to switch between foots. So whenever the event is detected, the line left_foot = not left_foot simply inverts the initial value of i.e if it was , then it becomes and vice versa.We commented out the line WINDOW.blit(DINO_STANDING, (30, DINO_Y_POS)). This is only a temporary measure. We'll fix it soonAlright, run your code ! your Dino should now be running for dear life:But, we don't want the game to start playing by itself whenever we start it. Actually, we want it to start only when we hit the key on the keyboard. So, I re-engineered the code to make this possible:Essentially, I nested a new loop inside the game loop. This new loop will control when the game starts playing: So now, when we start the game, everything stays static until the key is pressed, at which point, the game play resumes as usual. A few things to note:We introduce a new variable to control when the game is in mode and when it's not. This variable is then passed into the function and used to determine when to display a moving or standing dinosaur and also when the horizon should scroll leftward and when it should not.Also, we used two events at the two modes of the game so we can know when the user hits the or keys to start or stop game play respectively.Now with these changes, we're done. Run the program and see your Dino obeying all your commands:Congrats !!! you just successfully tamed a Dinosaur.View the full code for this stage of the project on GithubSo in this post, we learned a little bit more about events in , how to create a custom event, how to make an event repeat after a specified time period, and we achieved our goal of displaying the Dino and making it move on the canvas.In the next post, we'll explore how to make the Dino jump as it tries to avoid Cactus on the road. That would be a great idea, I think. But for now:

item.title: Florida Deploys Robot Rabbits To Control Invasive Burmese Python Population
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
An anonymous reader quotes a report from CBS News: They look, move and even smell like the kind of furry Everglades marsh rabbit a Burmese python would love to eat. But these bunnies are robots meant to lure the giant invasive snakes out of their hiding spots. It's the latest effort by the South Florida Water Management District to eliminate as many pythons as possible from the Everglades, where they are decimating native species with their voracious appetites. In Everglades National Park, officials say the snakes have eliminated 95% of small mammals as well as thousands of birds. "Removing them is fairly simple. It's detection. We're having a really hard time finding them," said Mike Kirkland, lead invasive animal biologist for the water district. "They're so well camouflaged in the field."
The water district and University of Florida researchers deployed 120 robot rabbits this summer as an experiment. Previously, there was an effort to use live rabbits as snake lures but that became too expensive and time-consuming, Kirkland said. The robots are simple toy rabbits, but retrofitted to emit heat, a smell and to make natural movements to appear like any other regular rabbit. "They look like a real rabbit," Kirkland said. They are solar powered and can be switched on and off remotely. They are placed in small pens monitored by a video camera that sends out a signal when a python is nearby. "Then I can deploy one of our many contractors to go out and remove the python," Kirkland said. The total cost per robot rabbit is about $4,000, financed by the water district, he added.

item.title: Escaping Your Java Habits in Python: Writing Clean, Pythonic Code
item.author: Aaron Steers
item.content:encoded:
As engineers, many of us migrate between languages. Fun fact: 20 years ago - what was the first language I was ever certified in? But now, a dozen languages later, I want to pull my hair out when I feel like I'm reading Java code inside a file that ends in a "" extension.If you’ve spent significant time in Java, it’s natural to bring those habits along when coding in Python. Unfortunately, some of those habits can lead to over-engineered or awkward code that doesn’t at all feel . Worse: your habits may be contributing to bugs, and (worse yet:) slowing down code review.Not to bring shame, but to improve everyone's lives: I think these patterns are worth calling out — especially for developers making the jump from Java to Python.
1. Overcompensating for Dependency Injection (DI) Java is not particularly strong at dependency injection (DI). Without additional frameworks (e.g. Spring, Dagger, Guice, etc.), it's actually super difficult. Unbenounced to many, DI in Python is super trivial, actually. To manage dependencies, Java developers writing Python often build of abstractions, factories, and injection systems where none are truly needed.When we carry this mindset into Python, we often over-engineer DI using generics, abstract base classes, and unnecessary indirection. While Python do this, it usually isn't needed - and our future selves will thank us if we keep things simple.Prefer passing simple functions, Callables, and Unions of types.Embrace Python’s duck typing: if an object behaves the way you need, you don’t need to enforce a generic type hierarchy.Use default arguments or keyword arguments for flexibility.The second version is shorter, clearer, and easier to maintain.
2. The “Everything Must Be a Class” Habit In Java, basically everything lives inside a class. Utility methods go into static classes. Even trivial helpers often get wrapped into objects because free functions aren’t idiomatic.When carried into Python, we end up with tiny, boilerplate-heavy classes that don’t add real value. For example, you might see a or class in Python, which is entirely unnecessary and adds unnecessary friction to your callers.Write standalone helper functions if a class is not truly needed.Use modules as namespaces (a Python file a container).Only create classes when state or behavior needs to be encapsulated, or when instantiating the object adds something meaningful to your workflow.Again, the second version is shorter, more natural, and easier to maintain.Best yet: the person reading or reviewing the code (via static code analysis) that calling the function is correct - no pre-knowledge of how to work with the class is needed in order to review the code. This is an area where Java classically fails in regards to code review and also for AI agent applications: the amount of context needed to review code or to create new code is much higher if you need to fully understand a class's structure. Compare this with helper functions: there's no "wrong way" to call a helper function; you only need to read the docstring and function signature (aka, use tooltips and Intellisense) to confirm if the function call is correct or not.
3. Overusing Interfaces and Abstract Base Classes Every service has an interface, every implementation must be bound to it. This enforces structure, but at the cost of boilerplate.Developers sometimes mimic this pattern with abstract base classes and heavy use of types, even for simple use cases.Use duck typing: if an object supports the methods you need, that’s enough.When structure matters, consider for lightweight contracts.The second method uses less code, and is easier to support.🤫 Don't worry: the type checker will always tell you if you call a method that doesn't exist on the class! Adding type checks to your CI means this is safe, with often 50% less code and a much more readable and maintainable implementation.
4. Verbose Builders Instead of Simple Keyword Arguments Builders are everywhere for object construction with optional arguments.Developers sometimes reimplement builder-style classes just to avoid long signatures.Use keyword arguments with defaults.For structured objects, use or models.Again, the Pythonic version requires 75% less code, while being more readable, more maintainable, and much less likely to have unexpected bugs creeping in over time.
5. Not Using Keyword Arguments When You Should Function calls are almost always positional (Java doesn't support keyword args), and IDEs enforce correctness through signatures and tooling, while your function and method args inevitable devolve into long list of fragile and hard-to-verify positional inputs.Ex-Java developers often continue to use positional arguments in Python - even for long, many-input functions. This makes code fragile and unreadable—especially during reviews, where it’s literally impossible to confirm correctness without knowing the implementation signature by heart.Use keyword arguments for clarity and maintainability. They make function calls self-documenting and trivial to verify.# Fragile and hard to review create_source(name, config, catalog, state)
# Clear and verifiable create_source( source_name=name, config=config, catalog=catalog, state=state, ) The latter is correct and clear to its reader, whereas the former is literally impossible to verify from the code alone. The Pythonic version takes <2 seconds to reach 100% confidence (if it passes lint checks: it's correct), whereas in the Java-esque version, there's almost ability to reach a high confidence at all. You are fully leaning on tests and type checks - and if of the arg types are the same, then you are 100% leaning on tests.🧠 Ironically: even though the Pythonic version has more characters, your eye passes over it , quickly and subconsciously confirming the implementation in 0-2 seconds, whereas your brain freezes or just gives up when trying to review the first version. Since you don't have access to docs with the order of the input args, your ability to code review that (admittedly shorter) code snippet is zero-to-nil - unless you suspect something is wrong with it.What's worse: our code changes and evolves over time. Named args ensure your code will break cleanly when it is broken. Relying on positional args is like setting a time bomb that you know will go off, but you just don't know when. 💣If you’re coming from Java:Don’t over-engineer dependency injection—Python’s simplicity usually covers most use cases.Don’t create classes for everything—standalone functions are fine (and often preferred).Skip unnecessary interfaces—use duck typing or protocols only if truly needed.Use dataclasses and simple constructors instead of builders.Prefer keyword arguments in function calls for clarity and reviewability.The beauty of Python is its flexibility and minimalism. Lean into that. By shedding some habits from Java, your code will not only more Pythonic but will also be easier to read, maintain, and extend.It's not about which language is better - it's about making your code readable, maintainable, and intuitive to leverage and support. In the age of AI, these can mean the difference between keeping up, or falling behind.
Why this matters for the future of AI In the future, AI will write much more of the code that we all write and maintain today - but it has the same limitations that we have: it is reliant on limited context windows and on context-clues to correctly read and interpret code. If you want to future proof your code in 2025 and 2026: write code that even a mindless robot can review and maintain for you.In other words, keep it Pythonic - regardless of whether you are writing Python, Java, or Kotlin. 😅

item.title: Building Robust AI Voice Agents for Seamless Automation
item.author: OnlineProxy
item.content:encoded:
The rise of AI-powered voice agents is reshaping how businesses handle customer interactions, with significant improvements in efficiency, reliability, and scalability. In this article, we will explore how to build and optimize AI voice agents, focusing on automated tasks such as appointment booking, lead generation, and customer support. The insights shared here go beyond the basics, diving into the techniques and architecture needed to create sophisticated systems that drive business growth.
The Evolution of AI Voice Agents: From Concept to Execution AI voice agents, once the domain of sci-fi, have become a cornerstone of modern business automation. These systems combine natural language processing (NLP), machine learning, and voice synthesis to handle a variety of tasks autonomously. The potential for AI agents to save time, reduce errors, and improve customer satisfaction is immense.But creating a high-performing AI voice agent involves more than simply plugging in a pre-built solution. It requires careful integration of various technologies, a deep understanding of conversational design, and a reliable backend to ensure the system operates seamlessly in real-world environments.At the heart of this process lies the idea of conversational flow. This design technique ensures that voice agents respond to user queries in a natural and efficient manner. By organizing the flow of conversation to anticipate user responses, we can minimize "hallucination" — the generation of irrelevant or incorrect responses — and improve the agent's reliability.
Tech Stack: Tools and Platforms for Building AI Voice Agents To build a robust AI voice agent, several tools and platforms are essential. Here’s a breakdown of the primary components:Voice Synthesis and Recognition: Tools like Eleven Labs and OpenAI’s language models power the voice generation and speech recognition aspects. These platforms allow the agent to "speak" in a natural, human-like manner while understanding and processing spoken commands.: Platforms such as Twilio or VAPI enable your AI voice agent to place and receive calls. These services provide virtual phone numbers, automatic dialing, and the ability to handle international calls. However, some platforms like Retail AI may have limitations for international communication, necessitating the use of a flexible tool like Twilio for broader geographic reach.: Systems like n8n (a popular open-source automation platform) and Retail AI’s agent tools help to create the necessary workflows for AI interactions. These platforms provide pre-built templates for common tasks, like booking appointments or confirming lead interest, which can be easily customized.: A reliable vector database like Pinecone stores the knowledge base for the agent, allowing it to retrieve accurate information during a conversation. Integrating these tools ensures that your AI voice agent can operate independently, accessing data from files or documents stored in cloud storage like Google Drive.
Key Considerations for Designing Reliable Voice Agents Building a functional AI voice agent is an iterative process that requires attention to several technical aspects. Here are key considerations to ensure your agent works as intended:: When designing an AI voice agent to interact with leads or customers, it’s critical to have access to dynamic data—such as the customer’s name, time preferences, or specific queries. Tools like HTTP requests in automation workflows allow you to pass this dynamic data between different systems. For example, when a customer fills out a web form, the agent should immediately receive and use that data to initiate a call or schedule an appointment.Conversational Flow Design: Effective conversation design is crucial for the success of AI voice agents. It involves defining the structure of interactions so that the agent can respond to varying user inputs logically. A well-crafted conversational flow can:Provide relevant answers quickly.Reduce the chances of frustrating, confusing, or irrelevant responses.By defining these flows, we ensure that the agent delivers responses that are not only accurate but also engaging, making customers feel heard and valued.Real-Time Availability and Calendar Integration: For appointment booking, integrating with a calendar system is essential. Using tools like Qualcomm and enabling built-in functions in platforms like Retail AI, the agent can check availability in real-time and make accurate bookings. By calling API endpoints to retrieve calendar data, the agent can suggest available time slots and finalize appointments autonomously.Emotion Recognition and Human Handover: Voice agents should be equipped to detect user sentiment and respond accordingly. If a user shows frustration or requests human support, the agent should seamlessly transfer the call to a live agent. This capability relies on emotional intelligence algorithms and ensures that the agent can handle sensitive conversations effectively.: Since AI voice agents often deal with sensitive information, ensuring the security of the data is paramount. By using SIP trunking and secure connections, businesses can ensure that conversations are encrypted and confidential, reducing the risk of data breaches or misuse.
Building and Customizing AI Voice Agents: A Step-by-Step Guide Let’s explore the process of setting up an AI voice agent for a use case such as .Setting Up the Automation Platform: Begin by choosing a suitable automation platform, such as or . These platforms provide the necessary tools to trigger workflows based on external inputs, such as form submissions. You can set up triggers for various scenarios, such as receiving a form submission from a website or a lead generation page.Configuring Telephony Integration: Once the platform is set up, integrate with a telephony provider like . Purchase a phone number, configure it for outbound calls, and ensure that the platform is correctly connected via SIP trunking. This enables your voice agent to place calls on behalf of your business.Designing the Conversation Flow: The next step is to design the interaction. In this case, the agent needs to:Confirm the user’s interest in booking an appointment.Present available time slots.Confirm the selected slot and finalize the booking.Using , create dynamic conversational templates that take the user's input (e.g., preferred time) and confirm the details. If a slot is unavailable, the agent can suggest alternatives.: Once the workflow is in place, test the system rigorously to identify any areas for improvement. Ensure that the voice agent handles edge cases—such as invalid inputs or unresponsive users—gracefully. Afterward, adjust the conversational prompts to make the interaction smoother.
Optimizing and Scaling AI Voice Agents The final step in building a reliable AI voice agent is optimization. As the system scales, it’s crucial to continuously monitor performance, refine workflows, and integrate additional features like knowledge bases or multi-language support. By leveraging analytics and user feedback, you can fine-tune the conversational models to ensure that they remain effective and efficient.Additionally, integrating post-call analysis allows you to analyze conversations and improve the agent's performance by summarizing key details and identifying potential areas for improvement.
The Future of AI-Powered Voice Automation As businesses increasingly rely on AI voice agents, it’s clear that this technology is not just a novelty but a game-changer in how we interact with customers. By combining cutting-edge technologies like conversational AI, telephony integration, and automation platforms, companies can build systems that operate autonomously, scale effortlessly, and provide superior customer experiences.However, the success of these systems depends on thoughtful design, continuous iteration, and a strong understanding of the tools and techniques that make them work. Whether you're building a voice agent to handle appointments, generate leads, or provide customer support, the key is to maintain focus on reliability, adaptability, and user-centric design.Embrace the future—AI voice agents are not just the next step in automation; they are the foundation of intelligent, scalable customer engagement.

item.title: Core Concepts: Decoding Human Language
item.author: Dev Patel
item.content:encoded:
Have you ever wondered how your smartphone understands your voice commands, or how spam filters magically identify junk mail? The magic behind these seemingly effortless interactions lies in Natural Language Processing (NLP), a fascinating branch of artificial intelligence that bridges the gap between human language and computer understanding. In essence, NLP empowers computers to process, understand, and generate human language. This article will delve into the basic concepts of NLP, demystifying its core principles and showcasing its transformative power.NLP tackles the inherent complexities of human language, which are far from the structured, precise world of computer code. To bridge this gap, NLP leverages several key techniques:
1. Tokenization: Breaking Down the Sentence The first step is often , which involves breaking down a sentence into individual words or units called tokens. This seemingly simple step is crucial for further processing.
2. Stop Word Removal: Filtering Out the Noise Many words, like "the," "a," and "is," don't carry significant meaning in context. These are called . Removing them reduces computational load and improves the accuracy of subsequent analysis.
3. Stemming and Lemmatization: Finding the Root chops off word endings to get to the root form (e.g., "running" becomes "run"). , a more sophisticated approach, considers the context to find the dictionary form (lemma) of a word (e.g., "better" becomes "good").
4. Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging: Understanding Roles POS tagging assigns grammatical roles (noun, verb, adjective, etc.) to each word. This provides crucial context for understanding sentence structure and meaning.
5. Word Embeddings: Representing Words as Vectors This is where the "magic" truly begins. represent words as numerical vectors, capturing semantic relationships. Words with similar meanings have vectors close together in vector space. A common technique is , which uses neural networks to learn these embeddings. The distance between vectors can be calculated using cosine similarity:Cosine Similarity = (A ⋅ B) / (||A|| ||B||)Where A and B are the word vectors, ⋅ represents the dot product, and || || denotes the magnitude (length) of the vector. A cosine similarity close to 1 indicates high semantic similarity.
6. Sentiment Analysis: Gauging Emotions Sentiment analysis determines the emotional tone of a text (positive, negative, neutral). This often involves training machine learning models on labeled data, using techniques like Naive Bayes or Support Vector Machines (SVMs). A simple approach could involve counting the frequency of positive and negative words.
Algorithms and Mathematics: The Engine Behind NLP Many NLP tasks rely on machine learning algorithms. For example, sentiment analysis often utilizes algorithms like: This probabilistic classifier calculates the probability of a text belonging to a certain sentiment class based on the frequency of words.Support Vector Machines (SVMs): SVMs find the optimal hyperplane that separates different sentiment classes in a high-dimensional feature space. The gradient descent algorithm is often used to find this hyperplane, iteratively adjusting the parameters to minimize the error. The gradient intuitively represents the direction of the steepest ascent of the error function; by moving in the opposite direction (negative gradient), we minimize the error.
Real-World Applications: NLP in Action NLP's impact is pervasive:Chatbots and virtual assistants: Powering conversational AI systems like Siri and Alexa. Enabling real-time translation between languages (Google Translate). Identifying and blocking unwanted emails. Generating concise summaries of lengthy documents. Analyzing public sentiment towards brands or products.
Challenges and Ethical Considerations Despite its advancements, NLP faces challenges: Human language is inherently ambiguous, making accurate interpretation difficult. NLP models trained on biased data can perpetuate and amplify societal biases. Processing personal data raises ethical concerns about privacy and security.NLP is rapidly evolving, with ongoing research focusing on:More robust and context-aware models: Addressing the limitations of current approaches. Making NLP models more transparent and understandable.Multilingual and cross-lingual NLP: Improving the ability to process and understand multiple languages.The basic concepts of NLP, while complex, are fundamental to understanding this rapidly expanding field. As NLP continues to advance, its impact on our lives will only grow, shaping how we interact with technology and each other in profound ways.

item.title: Mastering Go’s sync.Pool: Slash GC Pressure Like a Pro
item.author: Jones Charles
item.content:encoded:
Imagine you’re running a busy restaurant. Every customer gets a shiny new plate, but you’re tossing them out after each meal. The trash piles up, the dishwasher’s overwhelmed, and chaos ensues. That’s your Go program when it’s churning through memory allocations, stressing the garbage collector (GC). Enter , Go’s secret weapon for reusing objects and keeping your app lean and fast. 🏎️In this guide, we’ll explore how helps you reuse objects to reduce GC pressure, perfect for high-performance apps like web servers or logging systems. Aimed at Go developers with 1–2 years of experience, we’ll cover practical tips, real-world examples, and pitfalls to avoid. By the end, you’ll be wielding like a seasoned chef reusing plates to keep the kitchen humming. 🍽️How saves memory and boosts performance.Best practices to avoid common gotchas.Real-world wins from web servers to logging systems.
What’s sync.Pool, Anyway? Think of as a shared toolbox. You borrow a tool (object), use it, and return it for someone else. It’s a thread-safe way to reuse temporary objects, cutting down on memory allocations and easing GC strain. Perfect for stuff like in a web server or in a logging system.
How It Works (Without the Boring Bits) : Use to grab an object and to return it.: Built-in concurrency support means no extra locks needed.: The garbage collector might clear the pool during a cycle, so you may get a new object instead of a reused one.High-frequency, short-lived objects (e.g., buffers per HTTP request).Scenarios with heavy allocation, like logging or JSON encoding.Long-lived objects (GC might eat them).State-sensitive objects (unless you reset them carefully).Cuts memory allocations, slashing GC work.Boosts performance in high-concurrency apps.Simple API, no complex setup.GC can empty the pool, so have a fallback plan.You must reset objects to avoid data leaks.: is your go-to for reusing temporary objects in high-throughput Go apps, but you need to use it wisely. Let’s explore how to do it right.
Segment 2: Best Practices for sync.Pool
Best Practices to Nail sync.Pool 💪 Using is like borrowing tools from a community shed. You need to keep them clean, return them promptly, and not rely on them always being there. Here are four battle-tested practices to make shine.Set up your pool with a function to create objects when the pool’s empty. Keep it lightweight—think , not a database connection.: No function? Your app crashes on . Heavy logic? You’re back to square one with performance.: Initialize globally at startup for shared access across goroutines.Always reset objects after grabbing them to avoid data leaks (imagine serving food on a dirty plate 🤢). Use to ensure you return objects with .: Forgetting to reset or return objects leads to bugs or pool depletion.✅ Get object and type-assert.✅ Reset state (e.g., ).✅ Return with .
3. Lean on Concurrency Magic is thread-safe, so you don’t need extra locks. Its design minimizes contention, making it perfect for high-concurrency apps. Pre-allocate objects at startup to avoid initial hiccups.: Don’t assume objects always persist—GC might clear them. Low-concurrency apps may not need .: Pre-fill the pool in for a warm start.The GC might clear your pool, so design for it. Rely on the function for fallbacks and replenish objects during low-load periods.: Over-relying on the pool without monitoring can lead to unexpected allocations.: Use to monitor pool behavior and calls.: Initialize smart, reset diligently, leverage concurrency, and plan for GC surprises. Now, let’s see in action!
Segment 3: Real-World Use Cases
Real-World Wins with sync.Pool 🌟 Theory’s great, but let’s see save the day in production. Here are three scenarios with code, results, and lessons learned.
1. Turbocharging Web Servers : A web server creates a per request, spiking GC and slowing responses under load.: Reuse with , resetting and returning after each request.GC frequency dropped 30%.Memory allocations cut 30%.: Close to avoid leaks. Oversized buffers? Adjust capacity dynamically.
2. Streamlining Logging Systems : A logging system creates for each log, hammering memory and slowing writes.: Reuse with .Memory allocations halved.: Always reset to avoid mixed logs. Replenish pool under high load.
3. Optimizing Database Queries : Frequent creation of query parameter structs spikes GC in a database-heavy app.: Reuse structs with , clearing fields after retrieval.Query performance up 10%.: Clear slices explicitly. Encapsulate reset logic for complex structs.: delivers big wins in high-allocation scenarios, but reset carefully and monitor pool health.
Segment 4: Testing, Pitfalls, and Wrap-Up
Testing sync.Pool’s Impact 📊 Let’s prove ’s worth with a benchmark comparing with and without pooling.Run: go test -bench=. -benchmem: 123 ns/op, 64 B/op, 1 alloc/op, 12 GC/sec.: 85 ns/op, 0 B/op, 0 allocs/op, 8 GC/sec.: Use to profile real-world apps and simulate realistic workloads.
Common Pitfalls and Fixes 🚨 : Residual data from unreset objects causes bugs. : Always reset (e.g., ).: Using for low-frequency allocations adds complexity. : Profile with to confirm need.: Empty pool leads to new allocations. : Pre-allocate or replenish periodically. is like a trusty sous-chef, quietly optimizing your Go app by reusing objects and easing GC pressure. Stick to these principles:Initialize with a lightweight function.Reset objects and return them promptly.Leverage thread safety and pre-allocate for concurrency.Plan for GC clearing with fallback logic.From web servers (30% less GC) to logging (50% fewer allocations), delivers. Keep learning from projects like Gin or Zap, and profile with to stay sharp. Try in your next Go project, benchmark it, and share your wins in the comments! Got questions? Hit me up! 😄

item.title: Title: Unearthing the Past: The Fascinating Revelation of 5,000-Year-Old Neolithic Pits in Germany
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: Title: Unearthing the Past: The Fascinating Revelation of 5,000-Year-Old Neolithic Pits in Germany Description: In a groundbreaking archaeological find, a team of researchers in Germany has uncovered an array of ritual pits that date back to the Neolithic era. This astonishing discovery has shed light on the mysterious and haunting practices of our ancestors, providing us with a glimpse into the past that we could never have imagined.The pits, which were discovered in the state of Baden-Wurttemberg, are believed to have been used for various religious ceremonies and burial rituals. The team of archaeologists, led by Dr. Ingrid Schmidt, has been working tirelessly to uncover the secrets of these ancient pits, which have remained hidden for thousands of years.One of the most fascinating aspects of the pits is the intricate designs that have been etched into their walls. These designs, which are believed to have been created using stone tools, depict a range of symbols and motifs that are thought to have held great significance for the people who built them.The pits also contain a wealth of artifacts, including pottery, bone tools, and even the remains of animals that were sacrificed as part of the rituals. These artifacts provide us with a glimpse into the daily lives of the people who lived in the region during the Neolithic era, and offer valuable insights into their beliefs and practices.One of the most intriguing discoveries made by the team is the presence of human remains in some of the pits. These remains, which are believed to date back to the Bronze Age, suggest that the pits were used for both burial and ritual purposes.The discovery of these pits has sparked a renewed interest in the Neolithic era, and has opened up a whole new world of possibilities for researchers. The team is currently working to excavate the pits and preserve their contents, in order to gain a better understanding of the people who built them and the rituals that they practiced.As we delve deeper into the secrets of these ancient pits, we are reminded of the incredible diversity and complexity of human history. The Neolithic era, in particular, was a time of great change and innovation, as our ancestors transitioned from a hunter-gatherer society to one that was based on agriculture and settlement.The discovery of these pits is a testament to the ingenuity and resilience of the human spirit, and serves as a reminder that we are all part of a long and storied tradition that stretches back thousands of years. As we continue to explore the mysteries of the past, we are reminded of the importance of preserving our cultural heritage, and of the responsibility that we have to future generations to pass on the knowledge and wisdom that we have gained.

item.title: Title: The Secret to Longer-Lasting Lithium Batteries: Electrolytes with a Boron Twist
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: Title: The Secret to Longer-Lasting Lithium Batteries: Electrolytes with a Boron Twist In the ever-evolving world of technology, one of the most significant challenges we face is the need for efficient and long-lasting batteries. Lithium-ion batteries, in particular, have become a game-changer in powering our devices, but they still have their limitations. That is, until now.Scientists from China have made a groundbreaking discovery that could revolutionize the way we think about lithium-ion batteries. By adding boron to the electrolytes used in these batteries, they have been able to significantly extend their efficiency and lifespan.Electrolytes are the lifeblood of lithium-ion batteries, allowing the flow of ions between the cathode and anode. However, they can also be a limiting factor in the battery's performance. By adding boron to the electrolytes, the scientists were able to improve the battery's conductivity and reduce its resistance, leading to a significant increase in efficiency.But that's not all. The boron additives also helped to prevent the formation of dendrites, which are tiny needle-like structures that can form on the anode and cathode of the battery. These dendrites can cause the battery to short circuit, leading to a rapid decline in performance. By adding boron to the electrolytes, the scientists were able to inhibit the formation of dendrites, leading to a longer lifespan for the battery.The implications of this discovery are enormous. Lithium-ion batteries are used in a wide range of devices, from smartphones and laptops to electric cars and even spacecraft. By improving the efficiency and lifespan of these batteries, we could significantly reduce our reliance on fossil fuels and pave the way for a more sustainable future.Of course, this is just the beginning. The scientists plan to continue their research and explore other ways to improve the performance of lithium-ion batteries. Who knows what other secrets they will uncover?In conclusion, the discovery of boron additives to electrolytes is a game-changer in the world of lithium-ion batteries. By improving their efficiency and lifespan, we could unlock the full potential of these batteries and pave the way for a more sustainable future. So, keep an eye on this space, because who knows what other amazing discoveries are just around the corner?

item.title: Title: Promoting Safety in AI: A Call to Action from OpenAI and Anthropic
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: Title: Promoting Safety in AI: A Call to Action from OpenAI and Anthropic Artificial Intelligence (AI) has been advancing at an unprecedented pace, with new models and technologies emerging every day. While this rapid progress has brought numerous benefits, it has also raised concerns about the potential risks and ethical implications of AI. As such, it is crucial for AI labs to prioritize safety and take proactive measures to mitigate these risks.In this blog post, we will explore the recent initiative by OpenAI and Anthropic to promote safety in AI through cross-lab safety testing of their AI models. We will delve into the details of this initiative, its significance, and the potential impact on the AI industry as a whole.OpenAI and Anthropic, two of the leading AI labs in the industry, have recently announced their plans to collaborate on cross-lab safety testing of their AI models. This initiative aims to set a new industry standard for AI safety and promote transparency and accountability in the development and deployment of AI systems.Under this initiative, OpenAI and Anthropic will share their AI models with each other for safety testing. This will involve a comprehensive analysis of the models' behavior, performance, and potential risks. The results of this testing will be made public, allowing other AI labs and stakeholders to learn from and build upon this work.Significance of Cross-Lab Safety TestingThe significance of cross-lab safety testing cannot be overstated. By sharing their AI models with each other, OpenAI and Anthropic are taking a proactive approach to identifying and mitigating potential risks associated with AI. This initiative will not only help to promote safety in AI but also foster collaboration and knowledge sharing within the industry.Moreover, by making the results of this testing public, OpenAI and Anthropic are setting a new industry standard for AI safety. This will encourage other AI labs to follow suit and prioritize safety in their AI systems. Furthermore, it will help to build trust and confidence in AI, which is essential for its widespread adoption and integration into various industries.Potential Impact on the AI IndustryThe potential impact of this initiative on the AI industry is significant. By promoting safety in AI, OpenAI and Anthropic are helping to mitigate the risks associated with AI and build trust in the technology. This, in turn, will encourage the adoption and integration of AI in various industries, leading to significant economic and social benefits.Furthermore, by fostering collaboration and knowledge sharing within the industry, this initiative will help to accelerate the development and deployment of AI systems. This will lead to the creation of new jobs, increased innovation, and the development of new products and services that will benefit society as a whole.In conclusion, the initiative by OpenAI and Anthropic to promote safety in AI through cross-lab safety testing is a significant step forward for the industry. By setting a new industry standard for AI safety and fostering collaboration and knowledge sharing, this initiative will help to mitigate the risks associated with AI and build trust in the technology. This, in turn, will encourage the adoption and integration of AI in various industries, leading to significant economic and social benefits. As such, it is crucial for all AI labs to prioritize safety and take proactive measures to mitigate the risks associated with AI.

item.title: Seth Michael Larson: Draft SMS and iMessage from any computer keyboard
item.content:encoded:
If you're like me, you don't the ergonomics of writing long text messages on your mobile phone keyboard. We own an “Arteck HB066” Bluetooth keyboard for this use-case which works great and costs $45. But I'm not interested in today.What if I could write text messages, both SMS or iMessage, using any computer keyboard?This little tool does just that: write text messages in this browser window, and it'll generate a QR code which you can scan with your phone camera to send the message. If you are sending to multiple recipients, use a comma () to delimit the different recipient phone numbers. I recommend using international codes ( for the USA), but it appears to work at least on iOS without them.Don't know or don't want to type in your recipient phone number directly? Add a as the recipient, scan the QR code, and then fill in the recipients on your phone to use auto-complete from your contacts list.All data stays within the browser: your data is not processed, saved, or sent to any other server. If this tool is useful: bookmark the page for later use and let me know what you think.QR codes generated using qrcode-svg, licensed MIT and Copyright (c) 2020 datalog.
